facts.text: Pasadena Hills grew during the 1880s citrus boom, centered around a lake once called Lake Buddy, named after a pet bull from an 1830s cattle drive.
The area’s history stretches back thousands of years, with Native Americans using local agate coral for tools before Seminole villages like Toadchudka appeared in the 1780s.
Railroads sparked settlement in the late 1880s when the Orange Belt Railway—later part of the Atlantic Coast Line—made Pasadena a key station in Pasco County.
The 1926 Florida land‑boom collapse halted speculative building near Pasadena Hills, leaving abandoned lots and halted construction after the 1926 hurricane and 1929 fruit fly crisis.
A devastating freeze in 1894–95 destroyed citrus groves, causing nearby settlements such as Earnestville and Freedtown to be abandoned by the early 1900s.
